concepts.title: Refraction through a Prism
concepts.description: A prism is a transparent optical element with flat, polished surfaces that refract light. It is used to disperse light into its component colors and to change the direction of light.

concepts.title: Magnification of a Compound Microscope
concepts.description: The magnification of a compound microscope is given by the formula: Magnification = (Distance between the objective and the eyepiece) x (Magnification of the objective) x (Magnification of the eyepiece).

concepts.title: Focal Length of a Lens
concepts.description: The focal length of a lens is the distance between the lens and the point where parallel light rays converge after refraction through the lens.

concepts.title: Sign Convention for Lenses
concepts.description: Use the following mnemonic to remember the sign convention for lenses: 'Object is in front of the lens, it's minus, and if it's convex, it's positive too!'

concepts.title: Confusion between Convex and Concave Lenses
concepts.description: Students often get confused between convex and concave lenses. Remember that a convex lens converges light rays and a concave lens diverges light rays.

concepts.title: Unit of Focal Length
concepts.description: The unit of focal length is the meter (m). It is the distance between the lens and the point where parallel light rays converge after refraction through the lens.

concepts.title: Magnifying Power of a Microscope
concepts.description: The magnifying power of a microscope is given by the formula: Magnifying Power = (Distance between the objective and the eyepiece) / (Focal length of the eyepiece).

concepts.title: Total Internal Reflection
concepts.description: Total internal reflection occurs when a light ray hits the boundary between two media and is completely reflected back into the first medium, without any refraction or transmission of light into the second medium.
